title: Croque Madame
subtitle: The Parisian café classic — toasted ham and Gruyère with béchamel, topped with a fried egg
description: A croque madame is a croque monsieur — Gruyère and ham between buttered bread, spread with béchamel, grilled until golden and bubbling — with a fried egg on top. The egg is what elevates it from a snack to a meal. The béchamel is not optional; it is what distinguishes a croque from an ordinary toasted sandwich. Made properly, the inside is molten and cheesy, the outside is crisp, and the egg yolk runs into everything.
sourceNotes: The croque monsieur appeared in Parisian café menus around 1910. The croque madame (the monsieur with a fried egg) follows the same tradition. Both appear in French brasserie menus throughout the twentieth century and are documented in Escoffier-era French café cookery.

recipeTools.notes: For the béchamel — a 500 ml pan is sufficient.
Set to high — for grilling the assembled sandwich until the cheese bubbles.

glossaryTerms.definition: A classic French white sauce made from a butter-and-flour roux cooked with hot milk. One of the five French mother sauces. In a croque, it prevents the sandwich from drying out under the grill and adds a creamy richness.

ingredients.notes: Thick slices — or pain de mie
Grated — for inside and on top

steps: Make the béchamel: melt 15g of butter in a small saucepan over a medium heat. Add the flour and cook for 1 minute, stirring, until the mixture smells slightly nutty. Gradually add the hot milk, whisking constantly, until smooth. Simmer for 2 minutes until thickened. Stir in the Dijon mustard, season with salt and nutmeg. Set aside.
Heat the grill to its highest setting. Butter one side of each bread slice. Place the buttered sides down on a baking tray.
Spread béchamel on the unbuttered (top) side of all four slices.
Lay the ham and half the Gruyère on two of the slices. Top with the remaining slices, béchamel-side facing outward.
Spread a little more béchamel on the top surface of each assembled sandwich. Scatter the remaining Gruyère over the top.
Grill for 4–6 minutes until the cheese is bubbling and golden and the bread is crisp underneath.
Meanwhile, fry the eggs in a little butter in a frying pan — sunny-side up, yolk still runny.
Transfer each croque to a plate and slide a fried egg on top. Serve immediately.

content.text: is not optional — it is what distinguishes a croque from a plain toasted sandwich. It prevents the bread from drying under the grill, adds a creamy layer between the ham and cheese, and holds the Gruyère on top. Make a small batch and spread it generously.

Melt {{butter-unsalted}} of butter in a small saucepan over a medium heat. Add {{plain-flour}} of flour and cook for 1 minute, stirring, until the mixture smells slightly nutty. Gradually whisk in {{whole-milk}} of hot milk. Simmer for 2 minutes until thick and smooth. Stir in the mustard, salt, and nutmeg. Set aside.

Heat the grill to maximum. Butter one side of all four bread slices and place buttered-side down on a baking tray. Spread béchamel on the top of each slice. Layer {{cooked-ham}} of ham and half of {{gruyere}} of Gruyère on two slices. Top with the remaining slices, béchamel-side up. Spread the remaining béchamel on top and scatter over the rest of the Gruyère. Grill for 4–6 minutes until golden and bubbling.
While the sandwiches grill, fry {{egg}} of eggs sunny-side up in a little butter — yolk still runny. Slide one egg on top of each croque madame and serve immediately.
